claims.text: On June 21, 1947, harbor patrolman Harold Dahl reported seeing six donut-shaped craft drop molten metal over Maury Island in Puget Sound.

evidence.quote:
June 21st, 1947, Harold Dah was
patrolling around Mory Island with his
15-year-old son, Charles, a crewman, and
his dog, Sparky. They were looking for
stray timber

claims.rationale: Dahl's Maury Island report is a documented 1947 event central to UFO historiography. Whether actual craft were seen is contested — Dahl himself later recanted and some researchers regard the whole episode as a hoax. Moderate directTruth for the report existing, lower for its literal content.

claims.text: One of the craft wobbled and dumped hot metal and thin metallic strips onto Dahl's boat, burning his son and killing his dog Sparky.

evidence.quote:
m. Harold could see into it
through the windows.
>> Did he see my ex-wife in there? No. Do
you want to hear about the UFOs or not?
>> Sorry. Sorry. I'm so har resentment.
Hey, go ahead.
>> The wobble

claims.rationale: This follows Dahl's own account. Skeptics (FBI files, later researcher Kenneth Arnold) attributed the 'debris' to industrial slag from a nearby smelter; Dahl partially retracted the story. Moderate-low directTruth.

dependencies.rationale: Provides the concrete damages that define the Maury Island encounter story.

claims.text: The day after the Maury Island incident, a dark-suited stranger in Tacoma who already knew every detail of the event warned Dahl to keep quiet.

evidence.quote:
ust met history's first man in black.
The stranger in black sat across from
Harold at a diner in Tacoma. He ordered
breakfast, but he never touched it.
>> That's a power move. Men in bl

claims.rationale: This is the founding 'men in black' story in UFO folklore. The event is attested only by Dahl's own testimony. Given the later hoax admissions and the subject's established unreliability, the MIB encounter's literal truth is doubtful.

dependencies.rationale: Introduces the intimidation-aftermath narrative that frames the rest of the story.

claims.text: Pilot Kenneth Arnold, who had his own famous UFO sighting near Mount Rainier three days later, was brought in by Amazing Stories editor Ray Palmer to investigate the Maury Island case.

evidence.quote:
iction. Palmer asked Kenneth Arnold to
investigate. Arnold was a pilot and
deputy field marshal. He became famous
for his own UFO sighting near Mount
Rineer just 3 days after the Mory Island
incident. Arnold de

claims.rationale: Ray Palmer (Amazing Stories editor) did hire Kenneth Arnold to investigate Maury Island in late July 1947; Arnold's own June 24 Mount Rainier sighting is well-documented. Historical fact about the investigation setup.

claims.text: On August 1, 1947, Air Force intelligence officers Captain William Davidson and Lieutenant Frank Brown flew out of McChord with samples of the Maury Island debris on a B-25.

evidence.quote:
s. Their B25 flew out of
McCord Air Base at 1:30 a.m. 2 hours
later, the plane went down.
[Music]
Two hours into the flight, the B-25's
left engine caught fire. Davidson tried
to stabilize the aircraft and Brown
ordered the two cr

claims.rationale: The August 1, 1947 crash of a B-25 (44-29127) carrying Davidson and Brown near Kelso, Washington is on the historical record; the two investigators did die, the two enlisted crew did bail out successfully. Mainstream history, independent of UFO interpretation.

claims.text: The B-25 crashed near Kelso, Washington, killing Davidson and Brown; some accounts claim the UFO debris they were carrying went missing from the wreckage.

evidence.quote:
bomber crashed near Kelso,
Washington. Davidson and Brown died on
impact. Their bodies were recovered, but
the UFO debris was gone. And nobody
knows what happened to the evidence, but
some think it was taken by the

claims.rationale: The crash and deaths are fact. 'UFO debris was gone' depends on what was ever on board — most researchers (including Kenneth Arnold and the FBI) concluded the 'debris' was slag from a local smelter that was on the plane for routine disposal. Moderate directTruth for the missing-debris framing.

dependencies.rationale: Completes the B-25 flight's fatal outcome.

claims.text: Tacoma Times reporter Paul Lance reported the officers' names before the military released them and claimed an anonymous source said the plane was shot down with a 20mm cannon.

evidence.quote:
porting for the Tacoma Times,
Paul Lance released the names of the
officers killed before the military
announced them. He also reported that an
anonymous source told him the plane was
shot down with a 20 mm cannon.
>> Who shot it

claims.rationale: The early name-release is attested in contemporaneous Tacoma Times copy. The 20mm-cannon shoot-down claim is an anonymous-source allegation; the official accident report ruled engine fire/mechanical failure. Low-moderate directTruth for the sabotage framing.

claims.text: Reporter Paul Lance died suddenly of meningitis at age 29 shortly after his Tacoma Times reporting, and the paper folded a year later.

evidence.quote:
reporter, died of sudden
menitis at age 29. Doctors never found
the cause. The Tacoma Times folded a
year later. Fred Chrisman was recalled
to military service and sent

claims.rationale: Lance did die young; meningitis is cited in the Tacoma Times' own archive records. The implied 'tying up loose ends' causation is speculative. Mixed factual/interpretive content.

claims.text: FBI agent Jack Wilcox's memo to J. Edgar Hoover stated that Harold Dahl never actually admitted to a hoax — only that he would call it one to avoid trouble.

evidence.quote:
l agent
Jack Wilcox corrected Hoover. We have
the memo. Will Cox wrote that Doll
didn't admit to a hoax. He only said he
would call it a hoax to avoid trouble.
He'd rather be known as a liar than
someone who saw a UFO. Privately, he
still said the st

claims.rationale: The FBI memo (SMX/Security Matter X files, declassified) is quoted accurately. This is a primary-source document.

dependencies.rationale: Counters the popular 'Dahl admitted hoax' narrative, lending weight to the original sighting report.

claims.text: Fred Chrisman, Dahl's business partner at Maury Island, was a career disruption agent for the OSS/CIA who ran disinformation operations inside the US, per the Easy Papers.

evidence.quote:
them. The
Easy Papers exposed his real job, career
disruption agent. He monitored targets
and fed them disinformation.
Chrisman has served many years as an
active agent for the old OSS and later
the CIA. He is rated an e

claims.rationale: Chrisman's OSS/CIA ties are referenced in researcher Peter Levenda's compilation, but the Torbitt/'Easy' document is itself an unauthenticated intelligence memo of disputed provenance. Moderate directTruth.

claims.text: Guy Banister's New Orleans detective office at 531 Lafayette Street shared the 544 Camp Street address with Lee Harvey Oswald.

evidence.quote:
Banister's detective
agency was located at 531 Lafayette
Street in New Orleans. But the building
sat on a corner. It had another
entrance, another address, 544 Camp
Street. Now, this address is important
because it was the home base of the most
famous conspirator of all, Lee Harvey
Oswalt.
[Music]
This web of

claims.rationale: The 544 Camp Street / 531 Lafayette corner building connection is the most famous physical link in the JFK investigation literature. The Warren Commission and House Select Committee both addressed it. Documented fact.

claims.text: On November 12, 1963, Kennedy issued National Security Action Memorandum 271 ordering declassification of UFO intelligence files and sharing with NASA.

evidence.quote:
>> On November 12th, 1963, just 10 days
before his death, Kennedy issued two
memos. The first was National Security
Action Memorandum 271.
>> I have initiated a program for the
orderly declassification of all UFO
intelligence files affecting national
security. I have in

claims.rationale: NSAM 271 is a real document but its actual text concerns US-Soviet space cooperation generally, not 'UFO intelligence files'. The 'UFO disclosure memo' version widely quoted in conspiracy circles is from the disputed Majestic-12 document set, not NSAM 271 itself. The UFO-file framing is a conspiracy-lore embellishment.

claims.text: Fired CIA director Allen Dulles had overseen the UFO cover-up since 1947 and viewed Kennedy's disclosure push as treasonous.

evidence.quote:
To former CIA director Alan Dulles, who
Kennedy had fired after the Bay of Pigs
disaster, this wasn't diplomacy. It was
treason. Dulles had overseen the UFO
coverup since 1947. He'd investigated
the Mory Island incident. He knew what
technology the g

claims.rationale: Dulles was fired after the Bay of Pigs, that is fact. The claim that he personally oversaw a UFO cover-up from 1947 or investigated Maury Island is not supported by declassified records; the former is speculative framing common to MJ-12 lore. Low directTruth.

claims.text: Kennedy awarded the TFX (F-111) fighter contract to General Dynamics instead of Boeing, costing the military-industrial complex a major deal.

evidence.quote:
istory to General Dynamics.
Everyone expected it to go to Boeing,
the cornerstone of the
military-industrial complex. But because
of JFK, Boeing and its network of
powerful allies had just lost a fortune.
>> Especially all the people in Con

claims.rationale: The November 1962 TFX contract award to General Dynamics over Boeing is documented and was a major industrial-political event; Senator McClellan's hearings looked into the decision. 'Largest defense deal in history' is a slight overstatement but the award itself is fact.

claims.text: DA Jim Garrison believed Fred Chrisman was one of the Dealey Plaza shooters but could not prove it.

evidence.quote:
nd he believed
Fred Chrisman pulled the trigger, but he
couldn't prove it.
>> Couldn't prove it? The guy's connected
to CIA, UFOs, and the guy who got the
first phone call. Uh, what do you n

claims.rationale: Garrison's suspicion of Chrisman is documented in his case files and Oliver Stone's JFK. The subpoena and grand-jury dismissal are facts; the shooter allegation is Garrison's unproven theory. Omitting directTruth because the underlying shooter claim is unsubstantiated.

dependencies.rationale: Builds on Chrisman's established intelligence background to argue for his JFK-assassination role.

claims.text: RFK Jr. says his father privately called the Warren Commission report a shoddy piece of craftsmanship despite publicly accepting it.

evidence.quote:
Warren Commission report. But according
to his son RFK Jr., he privately called
it a shoddy piece of craftsmanship.
>> Shoddy? Uh, that's an insult to shoddy
craftsman everywhere.
[Music]
>> There's a 60-year cover up. They, you

claims.rationale: RFK Jr. has publicly repeated this attribution in interviews and his book. RFK Sr.'s private skepticism about the Warren Commission is corroborated by associates including Arthur Schlesinger Jr. and Frank Mankiewicz.

dependencies.rationale: A Kennedy insider's private doubt of the Warren Commission conclusion is consistent with the broader conspiracy thesis.
